# System Entropy

## The Physics

All engineered systems accumulate disorder over time. Dependencies drift out of
alignment, configurations diverge from their documented state, assumptions
embedded in code cease to reflect the environment in which the code runs.
Documentation decays. Interfaces ossify around obsolete contracts. Test suites
lose coverage as the system they guard evolves beneath them.

This is not a failure of discipline or craft. It is thermodynamic. Every system
exists in an environment that changes independently of the system itself —
operating systems update, libraries publish new versions, team members rotate,
business requirements shift. Each environmental change introduces a small delta
between the system-as-designed and the system-as-deployed. These deltas compound.

The rate of entropy accumulation is governed by two factors: the rate of
environmental change and the frequency of active maintenance. A system that is
never touched does not remain stable — it rots, because the world around it
moves on. Maintenance is not overhead; it is the energy input that keeps a
system in a low-entropy state.

## Why This Matters

- **Planning**: Maintenance is not optional rework — it is a first-class cost of ownership that must be budgeted.
- **Architecture**: Systems designed for maintainability resist entropy longer than systems designed only for initial function.
- **Governance**: Staleness detection, review cadences, and dependency audits are entropy-fighting mechanisms.
- **Risk**: The longer a system goes without active maintenance, the higher the probability that its next change triggers a cascade of failures.

## Observable Patterns

- Dependency update backlogs grow nonlinearly — the longer you wait, the harder each update becomes.
- Configuration drift between environments accelerates after initial deployment.
- Documentation accuracy degrades in proportion to the rate of system change.
- "It worked last time" becomes progressively less predictive as time since last verification increases.

## 1970/2070 Note

A 1970 mainframe operator watched JCL decks become invalid as system libraries
were updated beneath them. A 2070 quantum systems engineer will watch qubit
calibration profiles drift as hardware ages. The substrate changes; the
thermodynamics do not. Entropy is substrate-independent.

## Relationship to Meta Layer

- JOS-P13 (Small Reversible Changes) — small, frequent changes are an entropy-management strategy
- JOS-P19 (No Stale Information) — staleness is entropy made visible in the information layer
- JOS-C07 (Knowledge Decay) — the information-specific manifestation of this universal concept
